title: Upasarga-hara Stotra (Fourth Smaraṇa)
summary: The Upasarga-hara Stotra is a short, five-verse Prakrit hymn to Pārśvanātha, the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, whose very name means 'remover of afflictions' (upasarga). Devotees recite it for protection — from poison, disease, planetary troubles, fear and misfortune — but it gently lifts the reader from these worldly wishes toward the real goal: right faith and final liberation. It closes with a humble prayer not for safety but for bodhi (the seed of enlightenment) life after life. It is one of the nine protective 'remembrances' (Nava Smaraṇa) chanted in the fortnightly Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
ritualPlacement: This is the 4th of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa) — the fixed set of protective stotras recited within the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa of the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) tradition, after the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. Because Cāumāsī (Cāturmāsika) and Sāṁvatsarika pratikramaṇa carry no independent text of their own — they are performed 'as per Pakkhī' with only the word-substitution (પક્ખી → ચઉમાસી / સંવત્સરિ) and the kāyotsarga-count override — this Smaraṇa recurs unchanged in all three of those observances. It is not part of the daily Devasi or Rāi recitation.
authorAndSource: By tradition the Upasarga-hara Stotra is attributed to Ācārya Bhadrabāhu Svāmī, the Śrutakevalī and author of the niryuktis; tradition holds he composed it to invoke Pārśvanātha's protection (and that of the nāga-king Dharaṇendra) for the Saṅgha in time of calamity. It is a Prakrit (Ardhamāgadhī/Jaina-Māhārāṣṭrī) hymn in the gāhā (āryā) metre. The dedicatee is Pārśvanātha, whose iconography and lore link him to the serpent Dharaṇendra and the yakṣiṇī Padmāvatī — the background to its snake-and-poison imagery. The recension printed here is the standard five-gāthā text as it appears verbatim in the source book પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipakṣa edition).
structureArc: The five verses ascend deliberately from worldly protection to liberation. v.1 is the invocation: Pārśva is named the upasarga-hara, 'freed from the cloud of karma' (kammaghaṇa-mukkaṁ), the 'destroyer of the snake's poison' (visahara-visa-ninnāsaṁ), and the 'abode of all auspiciousness and welfare'. v.2 turns to the famous Visahara mantra: whoever ever keeps it 'in the throat' finds his planetary afflictions (gaha), disease (roga), epidemic (mārī), evil and aging all subside (uvasāmaṁ). v.3 then transcends even the mantra — 'let the spell stand aside' (ciṭṭhau dūre manto): a mere bow to you bears great fruit, so that beings, whether reborn as humans or animals, meet no suffering or misfortune. v.4 reaches the summit: once your samyaktva (right faith) is gained — a thing surpassing the wish-granting cintāmaṇi gem and the kalpa-tree — beings reach, unobstructed, 'the deathless, ageless place' (ayarāmaraṁ ṭhāṇaṁ, i.e. mokṣa). v.5 is the closing prayer: 'thus praised, O greatly glorious one, with a heart brimming with devotion — therefore, O Deva, grant me bodhi, birth after birth, O Pārśva Jinacandra.'
recurringImagery: Two motifs thread the whole hymn. First is a deliberate ladder of escalating refuge: the protective mantra (v.2) → a simple act of homage that already surpasses it (v.3) → right faith, samyaktva (v.4) → final liberation and the prayer for bodhi (vv.4–5). The poem keeps raising the value of what is offered, teaching that Pārśva's true gift is not safety but enlightenment. Second is the serpent-and-poison register native to Pārśva: he is the 'destroyer of the snake's poison' and bears the Visahara ('poison-removing') mantra — language rooted in his association with the nāga-king Dharaṇendra. Layered onto this is the stock Indic comparison of the supreme good to the cintāmaṇi (wish-fulfilling jewel) and the kalpa-vṛkṣa (wish-granting tree): right faith is said to exceed both, because those grant worldly wishes while samyaktva grants the deathless state.
keyThemes: protective recitation and the Visahara mantra
Pārśvanātha devotion and the Dharaṇendra/serpent lore
ascent from worldly boons to right faith
samyaktva surpassing the cintāmaṇi and kalpa-tree
prayer for bodhi birth after birth

glossary.definition: An affliction, calamity, or hostile disturbance; what the hymn (and Pārśva's name here) promises to remove.
'Poison-remover/snake-related'; the name of the protective mantra, and an epithet evoking Pārśva's power over poison and serpents.
A sacred protective formula; here the Visahara-mantra that v.2 says is kept 'in the throat'.
A planetary/astral influence held to cause harm; one of the troubles that 'subside' in v.2.
Epidemic or pestilence; listed among afflictions calmed by the mantra.
Reverential obeisance or bow; v.3 declares even a single bow to Pārśva 'highly fruitful'.
Right faith / correct insight (samyag-darśana); in v.4 the gift that surpasses every wish-granting marvel and opens the path to liberation.
The mythical wish-fulfilling jewel; a benchmark of worldly desire that samyaktva is said to exceed.
The kalpa-vṛkṣa, the wish-granting celestial tree; paired with the cintāmaṇi as something right faith surpasses.
The 'ageless, deathless place' — mokṣa, final liberation; the unobstructed goal of v.4.
The seed of enlightenment / attainment of right faith; what the closing verse prays for 'birth after birth'.
'Moon among the Jinas', a laudatory epithet for Pārśvanātha in the final verse.
A 'remembrance' — a protective stotra; this is the fourth of the nine Smaraṇas recited in the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
The nāga-king (serpent lord) who, in tradition, shelters and protects Pārśvanātha — background to the hymn's serpent/poison imagery.

howToRead: A newcomer can read it straight through as a prayer of protection that quietly redirects every wish toward enlightenment — notice how each verse asks for something higher than the last, ending not with safety but with bodhi. An advanced practitioner may attend to the deliberate renunciation of the Visahara mantra in v.3 ('let the spell stand aside') and the doctrinal weight of samyaktva over the cintāmaṇi/kalpa-vṛkṣa in v.4 as the hymn's true theological centre.

sources.title: પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત (Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit), Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim five-gāthā recension of the stotra, its placement as the 4th Smaraṇa within the Pakkhī pratikramaṇa, and the dedication to Pārśvanātha.

sources.title: Encyclopaedia Britannica — Bhadrabahu
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Bhadrabāhu Svāmī as an early Jain Śrutakevalī and traditional author of canonical/devotional works.

sources.title: Encyclopaedia Britannica — Parshvanatha
sources.consultedFor: Pārśvanātha as 23rd Tīrthaṅkara and his iconographic association with the serpent (Dharaṇendra), grounding the poison/snake imagery.

notes: Authorship is by long-standing tradition (Bhadrabāhu Svāmī) rather than by independent documentary proof; treat the attribution as traditional. The story that the hymn was composed in a time of calamity and invokes Dharaṇendra is devotional tradition and varies between accounts. Some recensions of the Upasarga-hara Stotra circulate with seven gāthās (two further verses regarded by some as esoteric or 'lost' and omitted in common practice); the source book here prints the standard five-gāthā text, which this preface follows. The Prakrit text returned by a generic web search was unreliable and was not used; verse readings here follow the book verbatim, including its Gujarati-script orthography.
